title: Introduction to Toxicology
summary: علم السموم (Toxicology) يدرس التأثيرات الضارة للمواد الكيميائية على الكائنات الحية، ويشمل آليات السمية والتشخيص والعلاج. العلاقة بين الجرعة والاستجابة محورية: 'الجرعة هي التي تصنع السم'. تُصنَّف التأثيرات السمية وفق عدة آليات منها نقص الأكسجين الخلوي وتثبيط الإنزيمات وتكوين الجذور الحرة. العلاج يقوم على ABCDE، وإزالة التلوث، وإعطاء الترياق المناسب.

key_drugs.name: Naloxone
key_drugs.note: ترياق التسمم بالأفيونات - يُعاكس تثبيط التنفس

key_drugs.name: Atropine + Pralidoxime
key_drugs.note: ترياق مبيدات الحشرات (الأورغانوفوسفات) - الأتروبين يحصر مستقبلات M والبرالدوكسيم يُعيد تنشيط AChE

key_drugs.name: N-acetylcysteine
key_drugs.note: ترياق الباراسيتامول - يُعيد تكوين الغلوتاثيون لتحييد NAPQI

key_drugs.name: Hydroxocobalamin
key_drugs.note: ترياق السيانيد - يرتبط به ويُحوِّله إلى سيانوكوبالامين غير سام

key_drugs.name: Deferoxamine
key_drugs.note: ترياق الحديد - يُخيِّل أيونات الحديد الحرة

key_drugs.name: Digoxin Fab
key_drugs.note: أجسام مضادة تُعادل الديجوكسين في حالات التسمم الشديد

key_drugs.name: Activated Charcoal
key_drugs.note: يمتز معظم الأدوية والسموم في الجهاز الهضمي ويمنع امتصاصها

mnemonics.title: AMPLE - تاريخ المريض المتسمم
mnemonics.body:
A = Allergies (الحساسية)
M = Medications (الأدوية الحالية)
P = Past medical history (التاريخ المرضي)
L = Last meal (آخر وجبة)
E = Events leading to exposure (ظروف التعرض)
إضافة: التوقيت والكمية وهل كان عمداً أم حادثاً.

mnemonics.title: ABCDE - نهج علاج التسمم
mnemonics.body:
A = Airway (المجرى الهوائي)
B = Breathing (التنفس)
C = Circulation (الدورة الدموية)
D = Disability (الوعي والعجز العصبي)
E = Exposure history (تاريخ التعرض)
هذا الترتيب ثابت في كل حالة تسمم.

mnemonics.title: قصة الترياقات - من يُنقذ من؟
mnemonics.body:
نالو (Naloxone) ينقذ من نام (Opioids).
أتروبين وبرالدوكسيم ينقذان من الفوسفور (Organophosphates).
نايتيل (N-acetylcysteine) ينقذ الكبد من سُمِّ الباراسيتامول (NAPQI).
هيدروكسوكوبالامين ينقذ من السيانيد.
ديفيروكسامين ينقذ من الحديد.
Fab ينقذ من الديجوكسين.

mnemonics.title: صورة ذهنية للجرعة والاستجابة
mnemonics.body: تخيّل منحنى S (سيجمويد): في البداية تزداد الجرعة بلا أثر (threshold). بعد العتبة كل زيادة صغيرة تُحدث تأثيراً كبيراً (slope شديد = نافذة علاجية ضيقة = خطر تسمم). الدواء ذو الـ LD50 المنخفض أشد سمية لأنه يقتل بجرعة صغيرة.

mnemonics.title: FLAP - آليات السمية الخلوية
mnemonics.body:
F = Free radicals (جذور حرة - مثل الباراكوات)
L = Lipid membrane disruption (تدمير الغشاء الخلوي)
A = Anoxia/Hypoxia (نقص الأكسجين - مثل CO وسيانيد)
P = Protein/enzyme inhibition (تثبيط الإنزيمات - مثل الأورغانوفوسفات)
إضافة: Immune-mediated و Genotoxicity.

flashcards.front: Define LD50.
flashcards.back: The dose that causes death in 50% of the experimental animals in toxicity studies. The lower the LD50, the more toxic the substance.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a Toxin and a Toxicant?
flashcards.back: Toxin: a naturally occurring poison produced by living organisms (plants, animals, fungi). Toxicant: a man-made or environmental chemical substance.

flashcards.front: What do NOAEL and LOAEL mean?
flashcards.back: NOAEL = the highest dose that produces no observed adverse effect. LOAEL = the lowest dose that produces the first observed adverse effect.

flashcards.front: What is the mechanism of toxicity of carbon monoxide (CO)?
flashcards.back: It binds to haemoglobin with greater affinity than oxygen, causing impaired oxygen delivery to tissues (cellular hypoxia).

flashcards.front: What is the mechanism of toxicity of cyanide?
flashcards.back: It inhibits cytochrome c oxidase in the mitochondrial respiratory chain, halting oxidative phosphorylation and ATP production.

flashcards.front: What is the mechanism of toxicity of organophosphates?
flashcards.back: Irreversible inhibition of acetylcholinesterase, causing accumulation of acetylcholine at neuromuscular junctions and nerve synapses.

flashcards.front: What is the toxic metabolite of paracetamol and what is the antidote?
flashcards.back: The toxic metabolite is NAPQI, which causes hepatic necrosis. The antidote is N-acetylcysteine, which replenishes glutathione.

flashcards.front: Which heavy metal accumulates in bones?
flashcards.back: Lead accumulates in bones and teeth, replacing calcium, causing long-term toxicity.

flashcards.front: What is a Toxidrome?
flashcards.back: A characteristic cluster of symptoms and signs produced by a specific type of poison, aiding rapid clinical diagnosis.

flashcards.front: List the methods of decontamination in poisoning.
flashcards.back: Activated charcoal, gastric lavage (rarely used), whole bowel irrigation, skin and eye washing, removal from the source.

flashcards.front: What is the precise definition of the dose-response relationship?
flashcards.back: A core concept championed by Paracelsus: 'The dose makes the poison.' Any substance can be toxic at a sufficient dose.

flashcards.front: What are the six branches of toxicology?
flashcards.back: Clinical, forensic, environmental, occupational, regulatory, analytical.

flashcards.front: What is the mechanism of enhanced elimination of toxins by urine alkalinisation?
flashcards.back: Alkalinising the urine with sodium bicarbonate raises urine pH, converting weak acids (aspirin, phenobarbital) to their ionised form, which cannot be reabsorbed.

flashcards.front: Why is Methanol more toxic than Ethanol?
flashcards.back: Methanol is metabolised in the liver to formaldehyde and then to the toxic formic acid, causing metabolic acidosis, blindness, and death.

flashcards.front: What are the essential laboratory investigations in poisoning?
flashcards.back: CBC, arterial blood gas (ABG), serum osmolality, toxicology screen, ECG, specific drug levels (paracetamol, salicylates, lithium, digoxin).

flashcards.front: What is the antidote for iron poisoning and how does it work?
flashcards.back: Deferoxamine chelates free iron ions, forming a complex that is excreted in the urine.

flashcards.front: What is the mechanism of ethylene glycol accumulation in the body?
flashcards.back: It is metabolised to oxalic acid, which precipitates in the kidneys as calcium oxalate, causing acute renal failure.

flashcards.front: Define Toxicology.
flashcards.back: The science that studies the harmful effects of chemical substances on living organisms.

flashcards.front: What is LD50?
flashcards.back: The dose that kills 50% of test animals — a measure of acute toxicity.

flashcards.front: What does 'The dose makes the poison' mean?
flashcards.back: Attributed to Paracelsus: any substance can be toxic depending on the dose; toxicity is dose-dependent.

flashcards.front: What are NOAEL and LOAEL?
flashcards.back: NOAEL: the highest dose with no observed adverse effect. LOAEL: the lowest dose with the first observed adverse effect.

flashcards.front: List the branches of toxicology.
flashcards.back: Clinical, forensic, environmental, occupational, regulatory, analytical.

flashcards.front: What factors influence toxicity?
flashcards.back: Dose, duration, and route of exposure; age, genetics, and co-morbidities; nutritional status; drug interactions.

flashcards.front: What is the specific antidote for paracetamol poisoning?
flashcards.back: N-acetylcysteine (replenishes glutathione).

flashcards.front: What is the specific antidote for iron poisoning?
flashcards.back: Deferoxamine.

flashcards.front: What is the specific antidote for organophosphate poisoning?
flashcards.back: Atropine + Pralidoxime.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is the correct antidote for opioid poisoning?
mcqs.explanation: Naloxone is a mu-opioid receptor antagonist that reverses the respiratory depression and reduced consciousness caused by opioids.

mcqs.q: What is the toxic metabolite formed from paracetamol in overdose?
mcqs.explanation: NAPQI (N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine) is the toxic metabolite of paracetamol that causes hepatic necrosis when glutathione stores are depleted.

mcqs.q: A patient exposed to pesticide spray presents with profuse secretions, convulsions, and miosis. What is the antidote?
mcqs.explanation: The symptoms indicate organophosphate poisoning (inhibition of AChE). Treatment: Atropine blocks muscarinic receptors; Pralidoxime reactivates AChE before 'ageing'.

mcqs.q: Which heavy metal accumulates in bones and replaces calcium?
mcqs.explanation: Lead is chemically similar to calcium and deposits in bones and teeth, causing long-term toxicity and neurological problems especially in children.

mcqs.q: What is the primary route of entry of carbon monoxide (CO) into the body?
mcqs.explanation: Carbon monoxide is a gas that enters the body by inhalation and binds haemoglobin to form carboxyhaemoglobin.

mcqs.q: What is the fundamental principle of the ABCDE approach in managing poisoning?
options: Focus on identifying the poison first
Assess and secure the airway, breathing, and circulation first
Perform laboratory investigations before any intervention
mcqs.explanation: The ABCDE approach prioritises patient safety (Airway, Breathing, Circulation) before anything else, because failure to address these vital functions promptly may be fatal.

mcqs.q: What is the most important investigation to confirm paracetamol poisoning?
mcqs.explanation: Measuring the serum paracetamol level with the time of ingestion determines the need for N-acetylcysteine based on the Rumack-Matthew nomogram.

mcqs.q: Which of the following accurately describes the concept of a Toxidrome?
options: A name for a group of toxic drugs
A characteristic cluster of symptoms and signs associated with a specific type of poisoning
A method for calculating the antidote dose
mcqs.explanation: A toxidrome is a characteristic pattern of symptoms and signs indicating the type of poisoning. Example: miosis + bradycardia + secretions = opioid or cholinergic toxidrome.

mcqs.q: What is the function of the Digoxin Fab antidote?
options: Antibody fragments bind free Digoxin to neutralise it
Competes with Digoxin at its receptor
mcqs.explanation: Digoxin Fab consists of antibody fragments that bind free Digoxin in the blood and neutralise its toxic effect on the heart.

mcqs.q: What is the fundamental difference between Toxicokinetics and Toxicodynamics?
options: Toxicokinetics studies the mechanism of toxicity; Toxicodynamics studies the movement of the poison
Toxicokinetics studies what the body does to the poison (ADME); Toxicodynamics studies what the poison does to the body
There is no difference between them
Toxicokinetics applies only to pharmaceutical drugs
mcqs.explanation: Toxicokinetics = what the body does to the poison (absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion). Toxicodynamics = what the poison does to the body (mechanism of action on receptors and cells).

options: The lethal dose for 50% of animals

mcqs.q: The antidote for opioid poisoning is:
mcqs.explanation: Naloxone is an opioid receptor antagonist.

mcqs.q: 'The dose makes the poison' is attributed to:
mcqs.explanation: Paracelsus — a fundamental principle of toxicology.
